title: 〜ていた
description: 〜ていた is the past tense version of 〜ている. This form puts emphasis on the duration of a past action.

content.text: Grammar Equations
Table of Contents
What Is ていた?

The ていた ending on verbs puts them into the past continuous form, which places focus on the duration of a past action. The form can take the shape of 〜 て いた or 〜 で いた, as in 食（た）べ て いた (was eating), 飲（の）ん で いた (was drinking), and 来（き） て いた (was coming). It's very close to the past continuous in English and can very often be translated as such, though there are some exceptions that we'll look at in a moment.

You've probably already come across the verb form ending 〜ている , which can be used to describe something that is happening now. So 食（た）べている means "is eating," and 話（はな）している means "is talking." This isn't the only use of 〜ている, but it is the one that is the most similar to English. The ていた form can have this same idea of an action being in progress, except that in this case the action is in the past. So 食（た）べている becomes 食（た）べていた and means "was eating."
ていた to Emphasize the Duration of an Action
Just like in English, one use of this form is to put the emphasis on the duration of the action:

content.japanese: 映画（えいが）を見（み）ていた。

content.text: In the top image, the action of watching a movie is contained within a certain timeframe and viewed as one unit. The duration of the action is not important. Rather, using the た ending emphasizes the fact that the action happened and is now complete.
In the bottom image, the use of 〜ていた makes it so that we don't know if the person finished watching the film or not. What is important is the duration of the action, rather than the result.
ていた for the Results of Actions

When we are talking about an action that was completed in the past but continues to be relevant afterward, we use forms like "had done" in English, as in "I had finished the essay before I realized it was on the wrong topic." Japanese doesn't have a structure like this, and instead 〜ていた can be used to express a similar meaning. Let's look at an example to get a better idea of what we mean here:

content.japanese: 朝（あさ）起（お）きたら、お母（かあ）さんがもう出（で）ていた。
content.english: When I woke up in the morning, my mom had left already.

content.text: If we translate "had left" into Japanese, we might be tempted to say 出（で）た here, since 出（で）ていた might seem like it means "was leaving." However, 出（で）た would indicate that my mom left at the specific moment that I woke up. Instead, Japanese uses the continuous form to show that something has happened, and the result remains relevant. So when we would use "had left" in English, we can use 出（で）ていた in Japanese.
Other Forms of ていた

Like the た form, this is the plain form of the verb, and it therefore does not show politeness. So when it is used at the end of the sentence, that sentence is casual rather than polite. You'll probably use it with friends and close acquaintances, and towards people who are younger than you or considered further down the social hierarchy for some other reason.
To make the verb polite, we can simply change the た ending into the ました ending.

On the other end of the spectrum, when we are speaking casually we very often contract the ていた form. In this case we don't say the い, and the ending becomes 〜てた. This is very natural in casual conversation, and you're likely to come across it a lot if you tend to speak Japanese with friends, or if you watch anime or read manga.
〜ていた can be used in the middle of sentences too. In this case, it doesn't show the level of politeness of the sentence. Instead, it gives information about the word or phrase that comes next.
When 〜ていた is used in the middle of a sentence, it is sometimes interchangeable with the present ている form, as in this example:

content.japanese: 日本（にっぽん）に住（す）んでいる時（とき）に、日本語（にほんご）を習（なら）った。日本（にっぽん）に住（す）んでいた時（とき）に、日本語（にほんご）を習（なら）った。
content.english: While I was living in Japan, I learned Japanese.

content.text: Both 住（す）んでいる and 住（す）んでいた can go before 時（とき）, without changing the meaning of the sentence.
Sources and Additional Readings

examples.japanese: 映画（えいが）を見（み）ていた。
朝（あさ）起（お）きたら、お母（かあ）さんがもう出（で）ていた。
examples.english: When I woke up in the morning, my mom had left already.

examples.japanese: 日本（にっぽん）に住（す）んでいる時（とき）に、日本語（にほんご）を習（なら）った。日本（にっぽん）に住（す）んでいた時（とき）に、日本語（にほんご）を習（なら）った。
